items.q: Does an artwork have a single fixed meaning set by the artist?
items.correct: No — meaning is shaped by cultural context, audience and time, so interpretations can differ.
items.misconception: Yes — the artist's intention is the only valid meaning.
items.random_wrong: No — artworks have no meaning at all.
items.why: Students assume intention fixes meaning, overlooking how contexts and audiences generate new readings.

items.q: Should art from other cultures be judged by Western fine-art standards?
items.correct: No — works should be understood through the conventions and purposes of their own context.
items.misconception: Yes — categories like 'fine art' apply universally to all cultures.
items.random_wrong: No — non-Western art cannot be analysed at all.
items.why: Students apply familiar criteria universally, misreading works made for different functions and traditions.

items.q: Why research an artwork's context rather than just describing what you see?
items.correct: Context explains why the work looks as it does and what it meant to its original audience.
items.misconception: Description alone is enough because everything is visible in the image.
items.random_wrong: Context only matters for sculpture, not painting.
items.why: Students rely on formal description and miss meanings that depend on beliefs, patrons and historical events.

items.q: 'Visual arts in context' primarily asks students to:
items.correct: Examine how cultural and historical contexts shape artworks.
items.misconception: Judge art only by personal taste.
items.random_wrong: Copy famous works as exactly as possible.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Examine how cultural and historical contexts shape artworks.. “Judge art only by personal taste.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Knowing an artwork's original function helps because:
items.correct: It explains choices of form, imagery and materials.
items.misconception: It proves the work is beautiful.
items.random_wrong: It always reveals the price of the work.
items.why: Correct answer: It explains choices of form, imagery and materials.. “It proves the work is beautiful.” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.correct: What beliefs and values of its society does the work reflect?
items.misconception: What was the artist's favourite food?
items.why: Correct answer: What beliefs and values of its society does the work reflect?. “What was the artist's favourite food?” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Comparing artworks from different cultures is valuable because:
items.correct: It reveals differing conventions, purposes and shared human concerns.
items.misconception: It decides which culture is superior.
items.random_wrong: It guarantees the works mean the same thing.
items.why: The answer examiners want is: It reveals differing conventions, purposes and shared human concerns.. “It decides which culture is superior.” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A contextual study compares 2 artworks from each of 3 different cultural contexts. How many artworks in total?
items.why: The real figure is 6 artworks. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
